primary_diagnosis.name: Widespread acute mechanical/bacterial (frictional-occlusive) folliculitis of the thighs, calves and buttocks following occlusive athletic wear and heavy sweating

differential.evidence_basis: Hyper-acute crop of monomorphic folliculocentric pustules, each centred on a hair with an erythematous halo and normal intervening skin, confined to the friction/occlusion zones of tight athletic wear after heavy sweating in humid conditions — the classic mechanism and morphology of occlusive/frictional folliculitis.

differential.diagnosis: Deeper staphylococcal soft-tissue infection (furuncle/carbuncle/abscess or cellulitis)
differential.evidence_basis: A widespread pustular staphylococcal process can progress to, or be accompanied by, a deep tender abscess or spreading cellulitis; must be actively screened for (deep fluctuant painful nodules, spreading erythema/heat, lymphadenopathy) because telehealth cannot palpate for it.

A widespread vesiculopustular eruption raises the triage-feared possibility of disseminated HSV / eczema herpeticum; distinguished by grouped/clustered painful vesicles or punched-out monomorphic erosions and systemic upset, none of which can be fully examined for remotely.

Also a pustular folliculitis, but follows spa/hot-tub/unchlorinated-pool exposure with a swimsuit-occlusion distribution; here there is no water exposure and the distribution tracks friction/sweat zones, making it unlikely.
Considered for pustules, but excluded by the hyper-acute 24-hour onset, exclusively monomorphic follicular pustules with no comedones, and the atypical calf/buttock distribution.
Excluded by the complete absence of systemic features (afebrile, no chills, no tachycardia, no malaise) and the strictly superficial, hair-follicle-centred nature of the lesions.

differential.diagnosis: Severe cutaneous adverse drug reaction (e.g. AGEP / other SCAR)
differential.evidence_basis: A drug-reaction pustulosis is essentially excluded because the patient is on no medications and has started nothing new, and the eruption correlates exactly with a mechanical sweat/friction trigger.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening the leading diagnosis is a frictional/occlusive folliculitis; the important-not-to-miss considerations are a deeper soft-tissue infection and disseminated HSV/eczema herpeticum, which drive the telehealth red-flag screen and the safety-net rather than the baseline tier.

differential.evidence_basis: No deep painful nodules/fluctuance, no spreading cellulitic erythema or lymphadenopathy, no fever or systemic upset, no grouped vesicles or mucosal sores, and no medications — a superficial, localised follicular process, not a deep, systemic, vesicular or drug-induced one.

differential.diagnosis: Deeper staphylococcal soft-tissue infection (furuncle/carbuncle/abscess or cellulitis)
differential.evidence_basis: Screened negative now (no deep fluctuant nodules, no spreading erythema/heat, no lymphadenopathy) but a foreseeable complication of a heavy pustular staphylococcal load — a mandatory safety-net target that telehealth cannot palpate for.

Argued against by the monomorphic hair-centred pustules (not grouped vesicles/punched-out erosions), absent mucosal involvement and systemic wellness, but retained as a safety-net for the vesicular mimic that cannot be fully examined remotely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The red-flag screen removes deeper soft-tissue infection, systemic infection and the HSV/SCAR mimics and confirms a telehealth/GP-manageable superficial folliculitis. Distress and extent do NOT justify same-day ED or 000.

differential.evidence_basis: Classic folliculocentric morphology and a clear occlusion/friction/sweat trigger; systemically well with negative deep-infection, systemic and vesicular-mimic screens; extent and severe itch drive symptom relief, an antibacterial wash, a topical antibacterial for the worst spots and trigger avoidance rather than emergency escalation.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The correct end-point is recognition of a common frictional/occlusive folliculitis, patient-performable measures (antibacterial wash, loose breathable cotton, shower/change immediately after exercise, do not shave the area), an oral antihistamine for the itch, a topical antibacterial for the most inflamed areas, and routine GP review if not settling — with explicit safety-netting for a deeper soft-tissue infection, systemic infection and disseminated HSV. Reflexive escalation to 000/ED is over-triage.

red_flags.description: Deeper soft-tissue infection — a furuncle/carbuncle/abscess (deep, tender, fluctuant nodules) or spreading cellulitis (redness and heat extending well beyond the follicular spots, lymphangitic streaking, regional lymphadenopathy).
red_flags.clinical_significance: A deep abscess may need incision and drainage and an established cellulitis needs oral (or IV) antibiotics and in-person assessment — neither can be palpated for over telehealth.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: An untreated abscess or spreading cellulitis can progress to extensive soft-tissue infection or systemic sepsis.

red_flags.description: Systemic infection / bacteraemia — fever, chills or rigors, feeling generally unwell, or rapid spread with systemic upset.
red_flags.clinical_significance: Systemic features on top of a widespread staphylococcal skin process signal invasive infection needing urgent in-person assessment and possible admission.

red_flags.description: Disseminated herpes simplex / eczema herpeticum (or another dangerous vesiculopustular mimic) — rapidly spreading grouped painful blisters or uniform punched-out erosions, mucosal (mouth/eye/genital) sores, or systemic upset.
red_flags.clinical_significance: Disseminated HSV / eczema herpeticum is a dermatological emergency needing antivirals and urgent assessment; a widespread vesiculopustular eruption must be screened for it, which telehealth cannot fully examine.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated eczema herpeticum / disseminated HSV can cause severe systemic illness and, with ocular involvement, sight loss.

pathophysiology_summary: Folliculitis is inflammation of the hair follicle, most commonly superficial and centred on the follicular ostium. In this frictional/occlusive (mechanical/bacterial) form, tight occlusive athletic wear plus heavy sweating in humid conditions macerates the skin, occludes the follicles and drives friction, damaging the follicular epithelium and allowing normal skin flora (chiefly Staphylococcus aureus) to invade the superficial follicle. The result is a hyper-acute crop of monomorphic 1-3 mm pustules, each precisely centred on a hair with a narrow erythematous halo and entirely normal intervening skin, distributed exactly over the friction/occlusion zones (thighs, calves, buttocks). The process is superficial and localised: there is no deep dermal abscess (no fluctuant tender nodules), no spreading cellulitis (no confluent erythema, heat or lymphadenopathy), and no systemic involvement (afebrile, well). Ruptured, excoriated pustules leave small honey-coloured crusts and are a minor portal for secondary deeper infection, which — together with the widespread vesiculopustular appearance mimicking disseminated herpes simplex — is why the presentation still mandates screening for a deeper soft-tissue infection, systemic infection and the HSV/eczema herpeticum mimic before committing to outpatient self-care. Management targets the mechanism (remove occlusion/friction/sweat, antibacterial wash, topical antibacterial for the worst spots) and the symptom (antihistamine for itch); it is highly recurrent if the mechanical triggers are not changed.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being swept along by 'hundreds of spreading pustules over the lower half of the body', unbearable sleep-preventing itch and the triage note's extensive-staph / widespread-herpes / severe-drug-reaction framing into calling 000 / sending to ED, when the red-flag screen for deep infection, systemic infection and the HSV mimic is negative and a superficial frictional folliculitis is telehealth/GP-manageable.

Treating a large number of pustules as automatically dangerous rather than recognising that a heavy but superficial folliculocentric eruption with normal intervening skin, no deep nodules and no systemic upset is benign; the folliculocentric morphology and clear mechanical trigger point firmly to a benign cause.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Missing the deep-infection and HSV screen
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Correctly labelling folliculitis but never asking about deep fluctuant lumps, spreading redness/fever, grouped painful blisters or mucosal sores — omitting the screen that separates a benign superficial folliculitis from an abscess/cellulitis or a disseminated HSV that telehealth cannot examine for.

Prescribing empiric systemic antibiotics for an uncomplicated superficial folliculitis with no deep or systemic features — over-treatment; oral antibiotics are reserved for furunculosis, abscess, cellulitis or systemic involvement.

Recommending a topical corticosteroid to settle the itchy 'rash' — a steroid on an infective folliculitis can worsen and prolong it; an antibacterial wash, a topical antibacterial and an oral antihistamine are the appropriate choices.
Failing to advise removal of the occlusion/friction/sweat driver — loose breathable clothing, showering and changing immediately after exercise, and not shaving the area — allowing rapid recurrence.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Direct examination of the pustules and skin to confirm the folliculocentric pattern and definitively exclude early disseminated HSV / eczema herpeticum
Palpation for deep fluctuant abscess nodules, warmth/induration of an early cellulitis, and regional lymphadenopathy
Exact blood pressure, heart rate, temperature and respiratory rate without a home device (source in-person values BP 122/78, HR 82, Temp 36.8, RR 16, SpO2 99%, Wt 75 kg)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a frictional/occlusive folliculitis from the exposure history and video; instruct patient-performable measures now (an antibacterial wash in the shower, loose breathable cotton clothing, showering and changing out of damp gear immediately after exercise, not shaving the affected area, an oral antihistamine for the itch); advise a topical antibacterial for the most inflamed areas; and arrange ROUTINE GP review (in person or telehealth) within about 5-7 days if it has not significantly improved, including to obtain the prescription-only topical antibiotic. Escalation to same-day in-person review or 000/ED is reserved for the red-flag safety-net triggers (deep painful hot lumps / spreading cellulitis, fever/systemic upset, or a rapidly spreading grouped-blister / mucosal HSV picture), not the baseline.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the diagnosis (the folliculocentric morphology and mechanical trigger are classic). The dominant risks are OVER-action (reflexively escalating a distressing but benign widespread pustular eruption to ED/000) and UNDER-screening the deeper-infection and disseminated-HSV mimics that telehealth cannot examine for.
